constraints: A strict strategic pause is unequivocally and immediately triggered due to critical pending specialist validation tasks concerning the recent systemd sandboxing implementations and boot time changes. The strict restrictions applied to core services have introduced significant architectural complexity and regression risks that require urgent specialist review before any standard development can safely resume.

The Architect is explicitly forbidden from implementing any new features, capabilities, enhancements, or making any modifications of any kind. Strictly zero code changes are authorized during this strategic pause, including bug fixes, structural refactoring, or superficial updates. Bypassing this for simple typos is strictly prohibited.

Do not modify any production code files whatsoever under any circumstances. Ensure the repository state remains completely pristine, entirely unaltered, and absolutely free of any architectural drift to give specialists a stable, unchanging baseline for their rigorous audits. This is essential for verifying the lack of sandboxing required on neos-liveuser-setup.service and validating ReadWritePaths configurations for services like neos-autoupdate.service.

All standard development, new feature work, and code optimization efforts are paused completely. They will only resume when comprehensive specialist validation confirms base system stability and clears the technical debt from the pending Sentinel and Palette tasks.

Systemd service validation by specialists must be completed before any further architectural work proceeds, specifically regarding the interaction between strict filesystem protection directives and script requirements.

Sentinel must conduct a mathematically rigorous audit to ensure ReadWritePaths correctly preserve access to critical directories (like /var/lib/pacman, /opt, /srv, /home/liveuser) and that necessary capabilities (like CAP_SYS_MODULE for dkms) are retained. Verify profile/airootfs/etc/systemd/system/neos-liveuser-setup.service strictly remains unsandboxed to allow operations such as useradd -m to persist without write failures.
